speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape number two of an interview with Danka Cyngler. At the end of the last tape, you were telling me about the time that your family was ordered into the Lvov ghetto. And then--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: So after when was quiet already, I heard everything settle down, I run out of the [INAUDIBLE]. And I ran-- I settled already a room where we should be. We were near [INAUDIBLE] six with another [INAUDIBLE]. We couldn't get ourselves a house. We had to share with another family.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I remember how we took a big-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] somebody was helping us to take this all things which is necessary, a bed, very necessary things. The rest we left all in the home and a lot of food.

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was a terrific organizer. He was so good. He prepared for us that we really didn't feel hunger. We had full bags of rice, full bags of [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] flours, sugar. Everything was so prepared, because he had a lot of leather he could change for food. You couldn't get anything to buy for money. Money wasn't important at that time. Only cotton, leathers, materials, those things you could change for food. One day, my father had a lot of friends in a little town that was like a farms. I went to the farm with another lady.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were already living in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were you free to come and go as you--

speaker: interviewee
text: I had to sneak out. And I was very good at that. Nobody could see me. I was skinny. And I used to go, like, without any--

speaker: interviewer
text: Star.

speaker: interviewee
text: --band. I supposed to have already a band, because-- I want to tell you something before that. Before we went to-- we still was in the Aryan side. And we all children were together. My older sister was 16 at that time. And one Ukrainian come. Then I felt anti-Semitism, because he call my sister. For so many meters, yards, you wasn't allowed to be without that band, Jewish [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Magen David. The Jews have to have a white band with blue Magen David, so everybody knows you're a Jew.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you start--

speaker: interviewee
text: The minute I arrived, they give us such an order.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yup.

speaker: interviewee
text: Everyone from 10 years have to wear it. And I didn't want to wear it. And I didn't look for 11 years or 12. I looked really young. I was skinny and not so brainy, maybe looked-- I wasn't stupid, only I didn't look nothing. Nobody did care for me. So I could be everywhere. And I-- he call her, because she was very pretty, very pretty, beautiful hair, always well-dressed. And he call her specially the few yards more, so she will be guilty. And he want to take her away. And my mother-- I saw what happened. So I called my mother. Mom, mom, come quick. Because that may wants to take away Ruszia.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my mother went out and took that man inside. And he smack my mother in the face, that Ukrainian. And I was kicking his legs, while he smacks my mother. Only I don't know if he felt myself. I was crying, why my mother had to be beaten? She's such a good woman. And she wants to give him some money to let her free. And something she did, because he let us free.

speaker: interviewee
text: And not long later, my sister went out and never come back, never. That was before we went to ghetto. So my family was very, very, very-- father and mother. My father, I thought he will be not able to take-- take. He was so depressed. My mother was depressed. We all, only parents more. Now, I am a mother, I understand how painful it could be for her.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we haven't got any more that sister. We are only three kids now. And we had to go to ghetto. And we went, we settled. We-- we didn't have a choice. What could we do? Do you know up until this day what happened to your sister, Ruszia?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no nothing. From that day, we never knew. We looked. We couldn't-- it's-- she's gone. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And we have to leave like it was. Because every day, was-- we get tragedies. Every day, somebody else was missing.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my parents couldn't forgive, forget till the last minute. I separate what I told you before. There start to be [INAUDIBLE]. And I take the kids. And we wasn't too long together, because they did it so fast, the Germans, so fast. The Ukrainians and the Poles-- they helped them.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then we felt the anti-Semitism, because in a normal life, you don't see those things. Everybody's nice, everybody greeting you, and normal. When comes problem, then you see who is your enemy. And we saw that we have a lot of enemies. And there were a few good people also. Because the way I went through, I had my father. He had-- had through it, because he knew a lot the Bible. And the monk were very in love with my father. They liked to talk to him, because he knew a lot-- a lot about this.

speaker: interviewer
text: [BACKGROUND NOISE] Just going to put it on pause for a second. We just had to stop for a-- a-- a few-- a minute about-- the phone rang. So it's been taken care of, so we won't have that interruption again. Before we stopped, you were talking about your life in the ghetto. Just continue, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. In the ghetto, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't remember a lot. Because I only remember when you had good neighbors. They come, and they let us know that and that day will be a big action and be ready.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what we did, my father was working on the Aryan side. And he organized for my brother, too. And for my mother, he arranged certificate that she doesn't have to work. She-- he buy a lot of money for Mom.

speaker: interviewee
text: And before I lost my sister Rusiza, my father had a special certificate that she doesn't have to work. He pay money, only probably they didn't-- they didn't worry for it.  It wasn't valuable, because she was taken with that certificate.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they didn't care about anything. Any-- you could have a million certificates. If I want to ruin our nation, that's all was in their eye. And they still exist, those intelligent people.

speaker: interviewee
text: I am not to hate anybody. Only you cannot forget, because this is very painful. That sits in me. And I try to love people, because I'd been in a family where we really did like people. I went to school. My mother gave me another sandwich in-- in case somebody's hungry to share, anybody. I had a-- an uncle which was very wealthy. He had import citrus. And every Friday, he used to deliver to all his sisters cases with fruit, oranges. And I remember now I went to school. Everyone-- doesn't matter who it was, Catholic, Ukrainian, it doesn't matter, I give them all shared oranges. They loved me for it. Because in my heart, never was anti-Semitism to hide somebody. And when that war come up, we saw how bitter it was for every Jew. There was something terrible. Anyway, we went back to ghetto. And everybody live for himself. The Jewish people were very bitter. Nobody could smile. It was very tough, because people didn't have food. Mother took away from a child. A child took away from mother, bread. There was shortage. And I didn't have this feeling, because we had food. My daddy prepared. We couldn't eat it, even. Because they got taken away. When I was jumped from a train, I come home, my sister told me that neighbors come in and took away all our food. [LAUGHS] So it was bitter for everybody. They thought we not alive, so they help themselves. And that was hard for us. Only we didn't-- we haven't been long in the ghetto, so we didn't worry about--

speaker: interviewer
text: How long, approximately, were you in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: I've been all the time in and out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because then I never come back. When I start already being on the Aryan side, after when it was the action, the two actions-- I don't remember when it was. I remember it was winter. And I never go back. And then they took us to the monastery.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Let's go back a little bit. You mentioned that they was an action in the ghetto. So can you explain what that meant for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I and my-- that was when I told you before, my father and I and my brother, we'd been the first guinea pigs. We was picked. The whole group people were in a pen, a bigger pen. And I hold everybody-- doesn't matter old, young. And I took them to Janowska-- not to Janowska, to a special place I don't remember. From there, to a train station, because they took us to Belzec. They want to burn us, to kill us. And everybody went. There was hundreds of people, hundreds, thousands. And everybody went like a sheep. We didn't have any ammunition to fight. We had to go. We only could jump. Or, they kill us. Or, we would be alive. That's what we did.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you please tell me about that? You--

speaker: interviewee
text: So I repeat, we went to the train. Did I say that before? We went to the train. And we were standing. My father was praying to God to help, and told me, don't worry. We will be surviving. You believe that they take us to work. We didn't believe that they going to kill us. Just suddenly somebody told us in the train we are going to get killed, not work.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then my brothers come up to me and said, look, [INAUDIBLE], I will throw you first. And you go back home. And I and Daddy, we will be back to you. You see.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I trust him. And I said, ask dad if he will be able to jump. He said was young. He was only 42. He wasn't an old man.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, I can see. I'm [LAUGHS] over 60, and I feel young. What about 42? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And they-- we went-- and he-- he threw me out from that little window. And I was-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I think it's a dream. It's not true-- it couldn't be like that, jump from a running train. And when the train pass, between, the Germans were shooting to people. They probably noticed that people jumping. And I was lucky. Didn't kill me-- just I knock myself in the knee. I couldn't walk. And when the train pass, people where killed, too. And some of the-- I don't remember what you call the people would work for the train. They was collecting people, or Jewish police. Somebody took me and took me right to Lemberg. Because it wasn't far from Lemberg. It was the first stop, maybe. And they want to give us to the ghetto. And one Pole or Ukrainian said, don't go. Child, don't go. Because they are killing-- they taking all children.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they took us to a big-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] it was a [INAUDIBLE], a big storage room. And they took us in there. And there was more kids.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we all were squeezing one another. And we survived the whole evening. Everybody was crying. And we didn't have any more energy to cry, even.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the morning, they came to collect the tools. Because that was a tool-- a tool storage. And a Pole said, you bloody Jew, what you doing here? Out from here, because I will give-- give you all away to the Germans. You shouldn't be here. So we separated. Everybody went in a different direction. And I remember my neighbor. She was such a nice lady, Mrs. Borisova.

speaker: interviewee
text: She took me in with such a warm heart and cuddle me. And next door was a lady, I remember now. Her name was Schwartz. She was a Polish and might be a Volksdeutsche.

speaker: interviewer
text: She come up to her. She said, what you going to do? You going to keep-- keep her here? We will be all killed. Don't keep her. She said, you go. And don't worry. I will not keep her.

speaker: interviewee
text: Only, I was with her for two weeks. And she saw me, too. Only she didn't react. She just frightened that the German will kill her. Because for one Jew, they would have a kilo sugar, or I don't know what. Anyway, I been there. And after this, every day she used to go to the ghetto to see if it's quiet. And one day she come up to me. She said, [INAUDIBLE], it's now quiet. You can go back to your home. So I went back. I was running, because I thought my mother was alive and my sister. I come home. Nobody's home, no one. It's like a revolution in the house. The house was robbed by people. Everyone was helping themself. Nothing was what to eat, nothing. And I start to cry. And then I heard a little voice comes up. It was my little sister. She was hidden by my mother under the bed, covered with all sorts of rags. And I drag her out. And we was cuddling each other and crying, and missing, and talking. And she was so happy that I come back, because no one was there. [CRYING] Sorry. [CRYING] And we start to be two girls, one looked after the other. I was seven years older than her. So she was six years of age, or five. And that-- suddenly, we heard-- we-- that daddy comes back. So we were so happy. And my brother come back. And I start to be the biggest girl. Because daddy was absolutely-- he couldn't walk. He was like, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] injured, very injured. Because when he jumped-- he wasn't old, only still wasn't so young like me.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I had to make him compress. And I took a sheet, and I make it wet. I didn't know what to do. Daddy told me, do this, do that. And I listened to him, and I looked after him.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then he told me what to cook. I had to do some cooking for them. And slowly, he recovered. In the bad time, everybody goes quick well. He recovered. And he believer, very strong believer in God. And he believed so strong that he wake up, and he said, we going to the Aryan side. And we will be there. We not coming back anymore here.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we planned. Mom was taken away forever. We never saw her again, such a lovely person. That's it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we start to work with the monks. And there was a Ukrainian director. And he didn't know that I am there, because I was hidden there. In the daytime, I was in a cellar with such big mouse and rats. I was sitting, frightened. Only, I didn't have a choice.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you-- sorry-- did you leave the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in that place where my dad worked. That was a very big house, factory. Only, they had a cellar. And this is a very old Roman buildings. And they had very old [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] places-- you know, rats and mouse-- mouse-- mouse-- mice and rats were keeping in such places.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your father was able to leave the ghetto every day to go to work?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Yes, for the beginning. Only then, there was a stoppage. We couldn't go back anymore. The second time that I told you, daddy came without my sister. That was the last time when we saw ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: I went. When daddy come to work without my sister, and I ask him, dad, where is [INAUDIBLE]? He said, he couldn't take her, because was a [INAUDIBLE] of children. So he left her in a special covered place. And he hoped that she will be there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when I heard this, I run. Because I loved her, and I didn't want her to not to be alive. When you're young, you think that you live forever.

speaker: interviewer
text: I run. And people used to stop me. Where are you running? I said, I have to run.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I run.  I went into the ghetto. Even the police in the front ask me why I go in. And so when I come into my house, the police was there, grabbed my sister, and me, too. And all children-- so many kids.

speaker: interviewer
text: He had to-- I said, why you have to keep me? Let us go. We got where to go. He said, no, I have to give you all-- so many kids to the Germans.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he took us to a big yard. And in that yard, all the kids had to stay till they had enough of them. And then they would take them to the train. And I told my sister, when they opened the gates, she stand next to me. And I push her. And I will run with her out of the ghetto. When they open the gates, and they all the time brought kids, children. So I didn't let them close the door. I went out. And Tova and [INAUDIBLE] with me, we run. The police let us run. And we run. And the ghetto had gates around, only from the ground was such place. The fence was so much room. So I move out. I lie down on the floor. And I move out. And I took her.

speaker: interviewer
text: We already on the Aryan side. And then stay on the back was a Ukrainian police or solider. He said, you Jewish, bloody Jew. What you doing? What you think you're doing?

speaker: interviewee
text: And you have to stand. And I'm going to kill you. Put your hands up. And he tries to do the rifle, and he wants to kill me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And before he wants to shoot, I ask him if I can ask him a question. Can you tell me, I ask him, what did I do to you? Why you want to kill me?

speaker: interviewee
text: And somehow, probably he maybe have children too, he didn't kill me. He let me go to that Jewish school where all children were. From there, they put us back to the train. And, again, to Belzec with my sister. That was my second time I'm-- I'm-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the train take us to Belzec. And I was-- the people was waiting in the queue to take out the clothes from there. Because the German wants to make sure that everything they collect from the people. And I already had experience, so I took my sister the way she was. And I was dressed. And I said, come with me. And we went quick to the train, because I knew we haven't got a choice. That is the one spot we can be. So we sat in the train. There was a bench. And I sat next to the window. And I sat her on my lap. And next to me was sitting a lady with a baby. And my Tova, my sister, noticed that she drank-- she kill her little baby. Because she didn't want the German to punish the child, so she thought this is better.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my sister start to cry and said to me, you will not do this to me, [INAUDIBLE]. I said, no, no. I will put you through the window. You will stay there. And I will jump straight away, and I will get you.

speaker: interviewee
text: How the train start to move-- I told people what to do, because I was already the expert, to take off this veil. And because it was such a-- veils. And I opened it. And I throw her. And I thought that her legs were taken under the train, and I lost interest to run. And I couldn't. I couldn't. I was frightened, because I already jumped, so I know the feeling. It's terrible. Only knowing that she's there, maybe she needs me. So I asked somebody to push me, and they did. And I couldn't find her. I was again knocked very strongly. And a Jewish police was catching-- when the train gone, the Jewish police went on. Because the German was killing. They saw people jumping. And when the train passed, the Jewish police was looking if somebody's alive. They took the dead people away. And they helped me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sorry. This is the end of tape two.
